aliases: Korean crispy pork with sweet and sour sauce

english: Korean crispy pork with sweet and sour sauce

food_subgroup: Korean-Chinese fusion meat dish; battered and fried pork with sweet and sour sauce and vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tangsuyuk. FOODON_03302051 (pork dish) is used as the closest parent category. A more specific term for Korean-Chinese battered fried pork with sweet and sour sauce does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Tangsuyuk. A0CKP (Pork meat, cooked) is used as the closest defensible parent. The dish includes batter coating, sweet and sour sauce, and mixed vegetables which are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Tangsuyuk or a sufficiently close Korean-Chinese pork dish. SNOMED CT anchor omitted.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Tangsuyuk is approximately 300 g, comprising battered and fried pork pieces tossed with sweet and sour sauce, woodear fungus, fruit, and vegetables, served as a shared main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Tangsuyuk (Sweet and Sour Pork, Korean style) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Pork, fresh, composite of trimmed retail cuts (leg, loin, and shoulder), separable lean and fat, cooked (FDC ID 167903) combined with USDA data for sweet and sour sauce components and batter coating; proportional recipe estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Tangsuyuk exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked pork (FDC ID 167903), starch-based batter (FDC ID 169726, cornstarch), sweet and sour sauce components (sugar, vinegar, soy sauce, ketchup), and mixed vegetables (woodear fungus, cucumber, pineapple, apple). Recipe proportions estimated as approximately 55% pork, 15% batter coating, 20% sauce, 10% vegetables and fruit. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for a restaurant-style Tangsuyuk. Confidence is medium due to estimation basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet and sour pork with rice — analogous estimate based on battered fried pork with starch coating and sweet sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Sweet and sour dishes with starch batter and sugar-based sauce estimated GI ~50-60.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tangsuyuk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on analogous starch-battered fried pork dishes with sweet and sour sauce. The primary glycemic drivers are the cornstarch-based batter (GI ~68 for cornstarch) and the sugar-rich sweet and sour sauce, moderated by the protein and fat content of the pork. A blended GI of approximately 55 (low category, GI <=55) is defensible per the methodology of Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 55.5) / 100 ≈ 30.5, using 300 g serving with 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; approximately 1140 mg per 300 g serving) from soy sauce, seasoning, and sweet and sour sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Tangsuyuk to small portions and request reduced-sodium sauce preparation, as a single restaurant serving may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~30.5) driven by starch-based batter coating and sugar-rich sweet and sour sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Tangsuyuk in small portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables or a low-GI side dish, as the high glycemic load from the batter and sweet sauce can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~42 mg per 100 g) from deep-fried pork, combined with high sodium from the sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Tangsuyuk to occasional consumption, as the combination of deep-frying, saturated fat, and high sodium contributes to cardiovascular risk; choosing a smaller portion and requesting sauce on the side can help reduce overall fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tangsuyuk (탕수육) is a popular Korean-Chinese fusion dish consisting of crispy battered and deep-fried pork pieces served with a glossy sweet and sour sauce. The dish originates from Chinese-Korean cuisine (Junghwa Yori) and is one of the most beloved dishes in Korean Chinese restaurants. The pork is coated in a starch-based batter — typically cornstarch or potato starch — and deep-fried until golden and crisp, then either tossed in or served alongside a vibrant sweet and sour sauce made from sugar, vinegar, soy sauce, and ketchup. Common accompaniments include woodear fungus, cucumber, pineapple, apple, and carrots, which add textural contrast and freshness. The Ajumma's restaurant version in Singapore incorporates a gochugaru-spice mix, adding a mild Korean red pepper heat to the classic sweet-sour profile. Nutritionally, Tangsuyuk is energy-dense at approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~11.5 g), significant fat from deep-frying (~9.2 g), and a notable carbohydrate load (~18.5 g) from the batter and sweet sauce. The dish is high in sodium (~380 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and seasoning. The glycemic load per restaurant serving is high (~30.5), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, Tangsuyuk warrants caution for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, glycemic load, and fat profile. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten. It is typically served as a shared main dish in Korean-Chinese restaurants.
common_user_queries: Is tangsuyuk the same as sweet and sour pork?
What is the difference between tangsuyuk and Chinese sweet and sour pork?
What sauce is used in tangsuyuk?
Korean sweet and sour pork nutrition

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Tangsuyuk was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked pork and individual sauce and vegetable components using proportional recipe estimation. GI is an analogous estimate based on starch-battered fried pork with sweet sauce, referenced to Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is systematic and defensible but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked pork composites; vitamin D is not expected to be a significant or reliably measurable contributor in this dish type and the source database does not provide this value for the reference ingredients used. All other per_100g values are numeric estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy component data. Nutrient values represent a plausible per-100g composite for restaurant-style Tangsuyuk and should be treated as estimates pending direct laboratory analysis. The Ajumma's Singapore version includes gochugaru spice mix which may slightly elevate sodium and add minor capsaicin-related metabolic effects not captured in the base nutrient profile. Halal status is listed as false because pork is the primary ingredient; this dish is not suitable for halal-observing consumers. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement of Tangsuyuk has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 30.5 for a 300 g serving.
